title: Solomon's Prayer at the Temple Dedication
shortDescription: Solomon's prayer of temple dedication from the First Book of Kings, asking God to keep his eyes open over the house where his name dwells. A royal plea for God's presence and attentiveness.
author: King Solomon
text:
Yet have regard to the prayer of your servant
and to his plea, O Lord my God,
listening to the cry and to the prayer
that your servant prays before you this day,

that your eyes may be open night and day toward this house,
the place of which you have said, 'My Name shall be there,'
that you may listen to the prayer
that your servant offers toward this place.

And listen to the pleas of your servant
and of your people Israel,
when they pray toward this place.

And listen in heaven your dwelling place,
and when you hear, forgive.

title: Late Have I Loved Thee
shortDescription: Augustine's moving profession of love from the Confessions — a sigh of relief at the recognition that God was always within, while he sought Him outside. A classic prayer of conversion and longing for God.
author: Augustine of Hippo
text:
Late have I loved Thee, O Beauty so ancient and so new;

late have I loved Thee!

For behold Thou wert within me, and I outside;

and I sought Thee outside

and in my unlovingness fell upon those lovely things

that Thou hast made.

Thou wert with me and I was not with Thee.

I was kept from Thee by those things,

yet had they not been in Thee,

they would not have been at all.

Thou didst call and cry to me

and break open my deafness;

and Thou didst send forth Thy beams

and shine upon me

and chase away my blindness;

Thou didst breathe fragrance upon me,

and I drew in my breath

and do now pant for Thee;

I tasted Thee,

and now hunger and thirst for Thee;

Thou didst touch me,

and I have burned for Thy peace.

title: Our Heart Is Restless (Great Art Thou, O Lord)
shortDescription: The opening of Augustine's Confessions, praising God's immeasurable greatness and singing of the paradox of the human heart, which is made to find rest in God.
author: Augustine of Hippo

title: Prayer for Peace (Lord, Make Me an Instrument of Thy Peace)
shortDescription: The peace prayer attributed to Francis of Assisi — a mission to carry love, forgiveness, hope, and light into a world of hatred, conflict, and darkness.
author: Francis of Assisi

title: Prayer Before Study (Give Me a Steadfast Heart)
shortDescription: Thomas Aquinas's prayer for a watchful, noble, upright, firm, and free heart that nothing can distract, debase, or lead astray from God.
author: Thomas Aquinas
text:
Give me, O Lord God, an ever watchful heart,
which no subtle speculation may lure from Thee.

Give me a noble heart,
which no unworthy affection can draw downwards to the earth.

Give me an upright heart,
which no insincere intention can warp.

Give me a firm heart,
which no tribulation can crush or quell.

Give me a free heart,
which no perverted or impetuous affection
can claim for its own.

Bestow on me, O God, understanding to know You,
diligence to seek You, and wisdom to find You;
a life which may please You,
and a hope which may embrace You at the last.

Amen.

title: The Prayer of Nicholas of Flüe
shortDescription: The short, powerful prayer of the Swiss patron saint Nicholas of Flüe — a threefold letting go and receiving: take what hinders; give what helps; take me from myself and give me wholly to you.
author: Nicholas of Flüe
text:
My Lord and my God,
take from me everything that distances me from You.

My Lord and my God,
give me everything that brings me closer to You.

My Lord and my God,
detach me from myself to give my all to You.

title: Nada Te Turbe (Let Nothing Disturb You)
shortDescription: Teresa's famous bookmark poem — a brief, powerful consolation: all things pass, God alone remains; whoever has God lacks nothing, even if alone.
author: Teresa of Ávila
text:
Let nothing disturb you,
Let nothing frighten you,
All things are passing away:
God never changes.

Patience obtains all things.
Whoever has God lacks nothing;
God alone suffices.

title: A Nun's Prayer (Prayer of an Aging Person)
shortDescription: A prayer attributed to Teresa of Ávila that names the weaknesses of aging with gentle humor — especially talkativeness — and asks God to be protected from them.
author: Teresa of Ávila
text:
Lord, Thou knowest better than I myself
that I am growing older and will someday be old.
Keep me from the fatal habit of thinking
I must say something on every subject
and on every occasion.
Release me from craving to straighten out everybody's affairs.
Make me thoughtful but not moody;
helpful but not bossy.
With my vast store of wisdom,
it seems a pity not to use it all;
but Thou knowest, Lord,
that I want a few friends at the end.

Keep my mind free from the recital of endless details;
give me wings to get to the point.
Seal my lips on my aches and pains;
they are increasing,
and love of rehearsing them is becoming sweeter
as the years go by.
I dare not ask for improved memory,
but for a growing humility and a lessening cock-sureness
when my memory seems to clash with the memories of others.
Teach me the glorious lesson
that occasionally I may be mistaken.

Keep me reasonably sweet;
I do not want to be a saint —
some of them are so hard to live with —
but a sour old person
is one of the crowning works of the devil.
Give me the ability to see good things in unexpected places
and talents in unexpected people;
and give, O Lord, the grace to tell them so.

title: Act of Confidence in God
shortDescription: An act of trust and surrender that professes firm faith in God's care for all who hope in him, and unconditionally submits one's own will to the divine.
author: Claude de la Colombière
text:
My God,
I believe most firmly that Thou watchest over all who hope in Thee,
and that we can want for nothing when we rely upon Thee in all things;

therefore I am resolved for the future to have no anxieties,
and to cast all my cares upon Thee.

I am determined to live without solicitude concerning tomorrow.
I mean to aim at being perfect in the fulfilling of each moment's duty,
and to leave all the rest to Thy good Providence.

I will not torment myself about my salvation,
but leave the care of that to Thee also,
doing what I can to serve Thee
and leaving to Thee the care of supplying for my negligence.

I shall not fear either demons or persecutions,
nor sickness nor death,
nor even hell itself since Thou art my God,
in whom I place all my confidence,
and what I hope to obtain from Thy infinite goodness
is the grace to persevere in Thy service to the end of my life.

title: Universal Prayer (Attributed to Pope Clement XI)
shortDescription: Pope Clement XI asks for the grace to recognize the transient as transient and the eternal as decisive — a prayer for right priorities in the brief earthly life.
author: Pope Clement XI
text:
Lord, I believe in you: increase my faith.

I trust in you: strengthen my trust.

I love you: let me love you more and more.

I am sorry for my sins: deepen my sorrow.

I worship you as my first beginning,
I long for you as my last end,
I praise you as my constant helper,
and call on you as my loving protector.

Guide me by your wisdom,
correct me with your justice,
comfort me with your mercy,
protect me with your power.

I offer you, Lord, my thoughts: to be fixed on you;
my words: to have you for their theme;
my actions: to reflect my love for you;
my sufferings: to be endured for your greater glory.

I want to do what you ask of me:
in the way you ask,
for as long as you ask,
because you ask it.

Lord, enlighten my understanding,
strengthen my will,
purify my heart,
and make me holy.

Help me to repent of my past sins
and to resist temptation in the future.
Help me to rise above my human weakness
and to grow stronger as a Christian.

Let me love you, my Lord and my God,
and see myself as I really am:
a pilgrim in this world,
a Christian called to respect and love
all whose lives I touch,
those in authority over me or those under my authority,
my friends and my enemies.

Help me to conquer anger with gentleness,
greed by generosity,
apathy by fervor.
Help me to forget myself and reach out toward others.

Make me prudent in planning,
courageous in taking risks.
Make me patient in suffering,
unassuming in prosperity.

Keep me, Lord, attentive at prayer,
temperate in food and drink,
diligent in my work,
firm in my good intentions.

Let my conscience be clear,
my conduct without fault,
my speech blameless,
my life well-ordered.

Put me on guard against my human weaknesses.
Let me cherish your love for me,
keep your law,
and come at last to your salvation.

Teach me to realize that this world is passing,
that my true future is the happiness of heaven,
that life on earth is short,
and the life to come eternal.

Help me to prepare for death
with a proper fear of judgment,
but a greater trust in your goodness.
Lead me safely through death
to the endless joy of heaven.

Grant this through Christ our Lord.

Amen.

title: Lord, I Know Not What I Ought to Ask
shortDescription: Fénelon's prayer of total surrender — the petitioner confesses his ignorance of what he needs, and entrusts himself entirely to the loving care of God, who knows him better than he knows himself.
author: François Fénelon

title: Serenity Prayer
shortDescription: The classic Serenity Prayer — asking for serenity for what cannot be changed, courage for what can be changed, and wisdom to know the difference.
author: F. Chr. Oetinger
text:
God, grant me the serenity to accept the things I cannot change,
courage to change the things I can,
and wisdom to know the difference.

title: Act of Love
shortDescription: Classic Catholic Act of Love — a profession of love for God above all things and for one's neighbor for the love of God, because God is the highest good and perfect love.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
O my God,
I love You above all things,
with my whole heart and soul,
because You are all-good and worthy of all love.

I love my neighbor as myself for the love of You.
I forgive all who have injured me
and ask pardon of all whom I have injured.

title: Act of Contrition
shortDescription: Traditional Catholic Act of Contrition — sins repented wholeheartedly out of love for God as the infinite good who was offended and abandoned by them.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
O my God,
I am heartily sorry for having offended Thee,
and I detest all my sins
because of Thy just punishments,
but most of all because they offend Thee, my God,
who art all-good and deserving of all my love.

I firmly resolve, with the help of Thy grace,
to sin no more
and to avoid the near occasion of sin.

Amen.

title: Benedictus (Canticle of Zechariah)
shortDescription: The canticle of Zechariah (Lk 1:68–79), praising God's faithfulness: he has visited his people, wrought redemption, and sent the dawn from on high.
author: Zechariah
text:
Blessed be the Lord, the God of Israel;
he has come to his people and set them free.

He has raised up for us a mighty savior,
born of the house of his servant David.

Through his holy prophets he promised of old
that he would save us from our enemies,
from the hands of all who hate us.

He promised to show mercy to our fathers
and to remember his holy covenant.

This was the oath he swore to our father Abraham:
to set us free from the hands of our enemies,
free to worship him without fear,
holy and righteous in his sight
all the days of our life.

You, my child, shall be called the prophet of the Most High;
for you will go before the Lord to prepare his way,
to give his people knowledge of salvation
by the forgiveness of their sins.

In the tender compassion of our God
the dawn from on high shall break upon us,
to shine on those who dwell in darkness and the shadow of death,
and to guide our feet into the way of peace.

title: Glory Be (Gloria Patri)
shortDescription: The short Trinitarian doxology giving glory to God the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — as it was in the beginning, so also now and always — the most frequent prayer of praise in the liturgy.
author: Traditional Liturgical
text:
Glory be to the Father,
and to the Son,
and to the Holy Spirit.

As it was in the beginning,
is now, and ever shall be,
world without end.

Amen.

title: Angel of God (Guardian Angel Prayer)
shortDescription: The classic Guardian Angel Prayer, invoking the personal guardian angel as a God-sent companion — for light, protection, direction, and guidance.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
Angel of God, my guardian dear,
to whom God's love commits me here,
ever this day be at my side,
to light and guard, to rule and guide.

Amen.

title: Prayer to Saint Joseph
shortDescription: Prayer to Saint Joseph as protector of the Redeemer and spouse of Mary — asking for his protection, intercession, and example as a father and faithful servant.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
Hail, Guardian of the Redeemer,
Spouse of the Blessed Virgin Mary.

To you God entrusted his only Son;
in you Mary placed her trust;
with you Christ became man.

Blessed Joseph, to us too, show yourself a father
and guide us in the path of life.

Obtain for us grace, mercy and courage,
and defend us from every evil.

Amen.

title: Prayer to the Holy Family
shortDescription: Prayer to the Holy Family as a model of true love — asking to learn from Jesus, Mary, and Joseph to love the truth and do good with joy.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
Jesus, Mary and Joseph,
in you we contemplate the splendor of true love;
to you we turn with trust.

Holy Family of Nazareth,
grant that our families too may be places of communion and prayer,
authentic schools of the Gospel
and small domestic churches.

Holy Family of Nazareth,
may families never again experience violence, rejection and division;
may all who have been hurt or scandalized
find ready comfort and healing.

Holy Family of Nazareth,
may the upcoming Synod of Bishops make us once more mindful
of the sacredness and inviolability of the family,
and its beauty in God's plan.

Jesus, Mary and Joseph,
graciously hear our prayer.

Amen.

title: Prayer to Saint Michael the Archangel
shortDescription: The prayer for the protection of the Archangel Michael in spiritual battle against evil — born from a vision of Pope Leo XIII, prayed as a classic prayer after Mass.
author: Pope Leo XIII
text:
Holy Michael, the Archangel,
defend us in battle.

Be our safeguard
against the wickedness and snares of the Devil.

May God rebuke him, we humbly pray;
and do thou, O Prince of the heavenly host,
by the power of God
cast into hell Satan
and all the evil spirits
who wander through the world
seeking the ruin of souls.

Amen.

title: Eternal Rest (Requiem Aeternam)
shortDescription: The classic prayer for the dead — asking for eternal rest and eternal light for the departed, that they may rest in God's peace.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
Eternal rest grant unto them, O Lord,
and let perpetual light shine upon them.

May the souls of the faithful departed,
through the mercy of God, rest in peace.

Amen.

title: Come, Holy Ghost (Veni Creator Spiritus)
shortDescription: Invocation of the Holy Spirit as Creator, Comforter, and Enlightener, who fills the heart, strengthens the weak, drives away error, and kindles hearts with the flame of love.
author: Hrabanus Maurus
text:
Come, Holy Ghost, Creator blest,
and in our hearts take up Thy rest;
come with Thy grace and heavenly aid
to fill the hearts which Thou hast made.

O Comforter, to Thee we cry,
Thou heavenly gift of God most high,
Thou fount of life, and fire of love,
and sweet anointing from above.

Thou in Thy sevenfold gifts art known;
Thou finger of God's hand we own;
Thou promise of the Father, Thou
who dost the tongue with power endow.

Kindle our senses from above,
and make our hearts o'erflow with love;
with patience firm and virtue high
the weakness of our flesh supply.

Far from us drive the foe we dread,
and grant us Thy true peace instead;
so shall we not, with Thee for guide,
turn from the path of life aside.

Oh, may Thy grace on us bestow
the Father and the Son to know;
and Thee, through endless times confessed,
of both the eternal Spirit blest.

Now to the Father and the Son,
who rose from death, be glory given,
with Thou, O Holy Comforter,
henceforth by all in earth and heaven.

Amen.

title: Magnificat (Canticle of Mary)
shortDescription: The Canticle of Mary (Lk 1:46–55) — a jubilant cry over God's greatness, who lifts up the lowly, feeds the hungry, and remains faithful to his promise to the fathers.
author: Mary
text:
My soul proclaims the greatness of the Lord,
my spirit rejoices in God my Savior
for he has looked with favor on his lowly servant.

From this day all generations will call me blessed:
the Almighty has done great things for me,
and holy is his Name.

He has mercy on those who fear him
in every generation.

He has shown the strength of his arm,
he has scattered the proud in their conceit.

He has cast down the mighty from their thrones,
and has lifted up the lowly.

He has filled the hungry with good things,
and the rich he has sent away empty.

He has come to the help of his servant Israel
for he has remembered his promise of mercy,
the promise he made to our fathers,
to Abraham and his children for ever.

title: Anima Christi (Soul of Christ)
shortDescription: The Ignatian Anima Christi prayer of union with Christ in all his dimensions — soul, body, blood, water from his side, passion, wounds, and eternal grace.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
Soul of Christ, sanctify me.

Body of Christ, save me.

Blood of Christ, inebriate me.

Water from the side of Christ, wash me.

Passion of Christ, strengthen me.

O Good Jesus, hear me.

Within Thy wounds hide me.

Suffer me not to be separated from Thee.

From the malignant enemy defend me.

In the hour of my death call me,

and bid me come unto Thee,

that with Thy saints I may praise Thee

for ever and ever.

Amen.

title: Sub Tuum Praesidium (We Fly to Thy Protection)
shortDescription: The oldest known Marian prayer from the 3rd century — a brief, pleading cry to Mary as the holy Mother of God for her protection and assistance in time of need.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
We fly to thy protection, O Holy Mother of God;

despise not our petitions in our necessities,

but deliver us always from all dangers,

O glorious and blessed Virgin.

title: Act of Spiritual Communion
shortDescription: The prayer of spiritual communion, in which the petitioner receives Jesus in the heart through faith, love, and longing when sacramental reception is not possible.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
My Jesus,
I believe that You are present in the Most Holy Sacrament.

I love You above all things,
and I desire to receive You into my soul.

Since I cannot at this moment receive You sacramentally,
come at least spiritually into my heart.

I embrace You as if You were already there
and unite myself wholly to You.

Never permit me to be separated from You.

Amen.

title: Sign of the Cross
shortDescription: The most fundamental Christian gesture of prayer — the Sign of the Cross as a profession of faith in the Trinity and in the redeeming cross of Christ, with which every prayer begins and ends.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
In the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.

Amen.

title: Our Father (The Lord's Prayer)
shortDescription: The prayer taught by Jesus himself (Mt 6:9–13) — the foundational prayer of all Christians worldwide, uniting all the essential attitudes of prayer within itself.
author: Jesus Christ
text:
Our Father, who art in heaven,
hallowed be thy name;
thy kingdom come,
thy will be done
on earth as it is in heaven.

Give us this day our daily bread,
and forgive us our trespasses,
as we forgive those who trespass against us;
and lead us not into temptation,
but deliver us from evil.

Amen.

title: Apostles' Creed
shortDescription: The oldest baptismal creed of the Church from the 2nd century — a compact profession of faith in God the Father, Jesus Christ, and the Holy Spirit.
author: Traditional
text:
I believe in God, the Father almighty,
Creator of heaven and earth,
and in Jesus Christ, his only Son, our Lord,

who was conceived by the Holy Spirit,
born of the Virgin Mary,
suffered under Pontius Pilate,
was crucified, died and was buried;

he descended into hell;
on the third day he rose again from the dead;
he ascended into heaven,
and is seated at the right hand of God the Father almighty;
from there he will come to judge the living and the dead.

I believe in the Holy Spirit,
the holy catholic Church,
the communion of saints,
the forgiveness of sins,
the resurrection of the body,
and life everlasting.

Amen.

title: Hail Mary (Ave Maria)
shortDescription: The core Marian prayer, composed of the Angel's greeting (Lk 1:28) and Elizabeth's greeting (Lk 1:42), together with a request for Mary's intercession at the hour of death.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
Hail Mary, full of grace,
the Lord is with thee;
blessed art thou amongst women,
and blessed is the fruit of thy womb, Jesus.

Holy Mary, Mother of God,
pray for us sinners,
now and at the hour of our death.

Amen.

title: Gloria (Glory to God in the Highest)
shortDescription: The great hymn of praise of the Mass liturgy from the 4th century, first sung by the angels at the birth of Christ, praising God's glory in heaven and on earth.
author: Traditional Liturgical
text:
Glory to God in the highest,
and on earth peace to people of good will.

We praise you, we bless you, we adore you, we glorify you,
we give you thanks for your great glory,
Lord God, heavenly King, O God, almighty Father.

Lord Jesus Christ, Only Begotten Son,
Lord God, Lamb of God, Son of the Father,
you take away the sins of the world, have mercy on us;
you take away the sins of the world, receive our prayer;
you are seated at the right hand of the Father, have mercy on us.

For you alone are the Holy One,
you alone are the Lord,
you alone are the Most High, Jesus Christ,
with the Holy Spirit, in the glory of God the Father.

Amen.

title: Nicene Creed
shortDescription: The complete Creed formulated at the Councils of Nicaea (325) and Constantinople (381) — the central doctrinal document of Christian theology and faith.
author: Council of Nicaea
text:
I believe in one God,
the Father almighty,
maker of heaven and earth,
of all things visible and invisible.

I believe in one Lord Jesus Christ,
the Only Begotten Son of God,
born of the Father before all ages.
God from God, Light from Light,
true God from true God,
begotten, not made, consubstantial with the Father;
through him all things were made.
For us men and for our salvation
he came down from heaven,
and by the Holy Spirit was incarnate of the Virgin Mary,
and became man.
For our sake he was crucified under Pontius Pilate,
he suffered death and was buried,
and rose again on the third day
in accordance with the Scriptures.
He ascended into heaven
and is seated at the right hand of the Father.
He will come again in glory
to judge the living and the dead
and his kingdom will have no end.

I believe in the Holy Spirit, the Lord, the giver of life,
who proceeds from the Father and the Son,
who with the Father and the Son is adored and glorified,
who has spoken through the prophets.
I believe in one, holy, catholic and apostolic Church.
I confess one Baptism for the forgiveness of sins
and I look forward to the resurrection of the dead
and the life of the world to come.

Amen.

title: Sanctus (Holy, Holy, Holy)
shortDescription: The threefold 'Holy' from the Mass liturgy (after Is 6:3) — the praise of all creation filled with God's glory, and a welcoming cry for the one who comes in the name of the Lord.
author: Traditional Liturgical
text:
Holy, Holy, Holy Lord God of hosts.
Heaven and earth are full of your glory.
Hosanna in the highest.
Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord.
Hosanna in the highest.

title: Agnus Dei (Lamb of God)
shortDescription: The threefold prayer to the 'Lamb of God' in the Mass liturgy — an invocation of Christ who takes away the sin of the world, with the plea for mercy and the peace of God.
author: Traditional Liturgical
text:
Lamb of God,
you take away the sins of the world,
have mercy on us.

Lamb of God,
you take away the sins of the world,
have mercy on us.

Lamb of God,
you take away the sins of the world,
grant us peace.

title: Te Deum Laudamus
shortDescription: The great hymn of praise from the 4th century, attributed to Nicetas of Remesiana — a thanksgiving prayer of the Church for creation and redemption, by which salvation history is glorified.
author: Nicetas of Remesiana
text:
We praise you, O God;
we acknowledge you to be the Lord.

All the earth worships you,
the Father everlasting.

To you all angels cry aloud,
the heavens and all the powers therein.

To you cherubim and seraphim continually cry:
Holy, holy, holy, Lord God of Sabaoth;
heaven and earth are full of the majesty of your glory.

The glorious company of the apostles praise you.
The admirable company of the prophets praise you.
The white-robed army of martyrs praise you.

The holy Church throughout all the world acknowledges you:
the Father of infinite majesty;
your adorable, true, and only Son;
also the Holy Spirit, the Comforter.

You are the King of glory, O Christ.
You are the everlasting Son of the Father.

When you took upon yourself to deliver man,
you did not disdain the Virgin's womb.

When you had overcome the sharpness of death,
you opened the kingdom of heaven to all believers.

You sit at the right hand of God in the glory of the Father.
We believe that you will come to be our judge.

We therefore pray you to help your servants,
whom you have redeemed with your precious blood.
Make them to be numbered with your saints in glory everlasting.

O Lord, save your people and bless your heritage.
Govern them and lift them up forever.

Day by day we magnify you;
and we worship your name for ever and ever.

Vouchsafe, O Lord, to keep us this day without sin.

O Lord, have mercy upon us, have mercy upon us.

O Lord, let your mercy be upon us, as our trust is in you.

In you, O Lord, have I trusted; let me never be confounded.

title: The Jesus Prayer
shortDescription: The Jesus Prayer of the Eastern tradition — the brief, constant invocation 'Lord Jesus Christ, have mercy on me' as a path to inner stillness and contemplation.
author: Orthodox Tradition
text:
Abide with us, Lord,
for it is toward evening
and the day is far spent.

Abide with us
and with your whole Church.
Abide with us
at the end of the day,
at the end of our life,
at the end of the world.

Abide with us
with your grace and goodness,
with your holy Word and Sacrament,
with your comfort and blessing.
Abide with us
when night comes upon us:
the night of affliction and fear,
the night of doubt and temptation,
the night of bitter death.

Abide with us
and with all the faithful
in time and in eternity.

Amen.

title: Salve Regina (Hail, Holy Queen)
shortDescription: The medieval Marian prayer from the 11th century — an invocation of Mary as Queen and Mother of Mercy, consoler of the banished in this valley of tears.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
Hail, Holy Queen, Mother of Mercy,

our life, our sweetness and our hope.

To thee do we cry,

poor banished children of Eve.

To thee do we send up our sighs,

mourning and weeping in this valley of tears.

Turn then, most gracious advocate,

thine eyes of mercy toward us,

and after this our exile,

show unto us the blessed fruit of thy womb, Jesus.

O clement, O loving, O sweet Virgin Mary.

title: Regina Caeli (Queen of Heaven)
shortDescription: The Easter Marian prayer from the 12th century, which replaces the Angelus during the Easter season and calls Mary to rejoice joyfully over the Resurrection of Christ.
author: Traditional Catholic
text:
Queen of Heaven, rejoice, alleluia.

For He whom you did merit to bear, alleluia,

has risen, as he said, alleluia.

Pray for us to God, alleluia.

Rejoice and be glad, O Virgin Mary, alleluia.

For the Lord has truly risen, alleluia.

O God, who gave joy to the world

through the resurrection of Thy Son, our Lord Jesus Christ,

grant we beseech Thee,

that through the intercession of the Virgin Mary, His Mother,

we may obtain the joys of everlasting life.

Through the same Christ our Lord.

Amen.

title: Memorare
shortDescription: The prayer of trust to Mary attributed to Saint Bernard of Clairvaux — with the confidence that no one has ever been left unanswered who fled to her in need and sought her protection.
author: Saint Bernard of Clairvaux
text:
Remember, O most gracious Virgin Mary,
that never was it known
that anyone who fled to thy protection,
implored thy help, or sought thy intercession,
was left unaided.

Inspired by this confidence,
I fly unto thee, O Virgin of virgins, my Mother.

To thee do I come, before thee I stand,
sinful and sorrowful.

O Mother of the Word Incarnate,
despise not my petitions,
but in thy mercy hear and answer me.

Amen.

title: Celtic Blessing (God Before Me)
shortDescription: An early Christian prayer of accompaniment from the 4th century, asking God to be an all-encompassing companion — before the petitioner, beside him, behind him, above and below him.
author: Celtic Christian Tradition
text:
God before me to lead me,
God behind me to guard me,
God beside me to befriend me,
God beneath me to support me,
God above me to bless me,
God within me to strengthen me.

title: Aaron's Blessing (The Priestly Blessing)
shortDescription: The Aaronic Blessing from the Book of Numbers (Num 6:24–26) — God's threefold blessing: protection, the shining of his face, and the peace he bestows.
author: Aaron

title: Lord, As Thou Wilt (Prayer of Bl. Rupert Mayer SJ)
shortDescription: The simple prayer of surrender by Blessed Father Rupert Mayer SJ — a threefold yes to God's will: as you will it shall happen, as you will it I will go.
author: Father Rupert Mayer SJ

title: Prayer for Good Humor
shortDescription: The humorous prayer of martyr Thomas More for health, good humor, and a sense of life's lightness — without self-pity and with trust in God.
author: Saint Thomas More
text:
Grant me, O Lord, good digestion,
and also something to digest.

Grant me a healthy body,
and the necessary good humor to maintain it.

Grant me a simple soul
that knows to treasure all that is good
and that doesn't frighten easily at the sight of evil,
but rather finds the means to put things back in their place.

Give me a soul that knows not boredom,
grumbles, sighs and laments,
nor excess of stress,
because of that obstructing thing called 'I.'

Grant me, O Lord, a sense of good humor.

Allow me the grace to be able to take a joke,
to discover in life a bit of joy,
and to be able to share it with others.

title: Prayer for Peace (Peace Prayer of St. Francis)
shortDescription: The universal peace prayer of the American poet Stephen Vincent Benét — asking that the small earth be made into a planet of peace, free from hatred and armament.
author: Stephen Vincent Benét
text:
God of the free,
we pledge our hearts and lives today
to the cause of all free mankind.

Grant us victory over the tyrants
who would enslave all free men and nations.
Grant us faith and understanding
to cherish all those who fight for freedom
as if they were our brothers.
Grant us brotherhood in hope and union,
not only for the space of this bitter war,
but for the days to come
which shall and must unite all the children of earth.

Our earth is but a small star in the great universe.
Yet of it we can make, if we choose,
a planet unvexed by war,
untroubled by hunger or fear,
undivided by senseless distinctions of race, color or theory.
Grant us that courage and foreseeing to begin this task today
that our children and our children's children
may be proud of the name of man.

The spirit of man has awakened
and the soul of man has gone forth.
Grant us the wisdom and the vision
to comprehend the greatness of man's spirit
that suffers and endures so hugely
for a goal beyond his own brief span.
Grant us honor for our dead who died in the faith,
honor for our living who work and strive for the faith,
redemption and security for all captive lands and peoples.
Grant us patience with the deluded
and pity for the betrayed.
And grant us the skill and valor
that shall cleanse the world of oppression
and the old base doctrine
that the strong must eat the weak
because they are strong.

Yet most of all grant us brotherhood,
not only for this day but for all our years —
a brotherhood not of words but of acts and deeds.
We are all of us children of earth —
grant us that simple knowledge.
If our brothers are oppressed, then we are oppressed.
If they hunger we hunger.
If their freedom is taken away
our freedom is not secure.
Grant us a common faith
that man shall know bread and peace —
that he shall know justice and righteousness,
freedom and security,
an equal opportunity and an equal chance to do his best,
not only in our own lands,
but throughout the world.
And in that faith let us march
toward the clean world our hands can make.

Amen.

title: Canticle of the Sun
shortDescription: Saint Francis of Assisi's great canticle of praise to creation — Brother Sun, Sister Moon, Brother Wind and Sister Water, and the brotherly Sister Death as bearers of God's praise.
author: Saint Francis of Assisi
text:
Most high, all powerful, all good Lord!

All praise is Yours, all glory, all honor, and all blessing.

To You, alone, Most High, do they belong.

No mortal lips are worthy to pronounce Your name.

Be praised, my Lord, through all Your creatures,

especially through my lord Brother Sun,

who brings the day; and You give light through him.

And he is beautiful and radiant in all his splendor!

Of You, Most High, he bears the likeness.

Be praised, my Lord, through Sister Moon and the stars;

in the heavens You have made them bright, precious and beautiful.

Be praised, my Lord, through Brothers Wind and Air,

and clouds and storms, and all the weather,

through which You give Your creatures sustenance.

Be praised, my Lord, through Sister Water;

she is very useful, and humble, and precious, and pure.

Be praised, my Lord, through Brother Fire,

through whom You brighten the night.

He is beautiful and cheerful, and powerful and strong.

Be praised, my Lord, through our sister Mother Earth,

who feeds us and rules us,

and produces various fruits with colored flowers and herbs.

Be praised, my Lord, through those who forgive for love of You;

through those who endure sickness and trial.

Happy those who endure in peace,

for by You, Most High, they will be crowned.

Be praised, my Lord, through our sister Bodily Death,

from whose embrace no living person can escape.

Woe to those who die in mortal sin!

Happy those she finds doing Your most holy will.

The second death can do no harm to them.

Praise and bless my Lord, and give thanks,

and serve Him with great humility.

title: Luther's Morning Prayer
shortDescription: Luther's classic morning prayer from the Small Catechism — thanksgiving for protection through the night, asking God's protection for the new day and entrusting body and soul into God's hands.
author: Martin Luther
text:
I thank You, my heavenly Father,
through Jesus Christ, Your dear Son,
that You have kept me this night from all harm and danger;

and I pray that You would keep me this day also from sin and every evil,
that all my doings and life may please You.

For into Your hands I commend myself,
my body and soul, and all things.

Let Your holy angel be with me,
that the evil foe may have no power over me.

Amen.

title: Elijah's Prayer on Mount Carmel
shortDescription: Elijah's prayer from the Old Testament – a fervent plea for God to reveal Himself as the true God to Israel, demonstrating His power and turning their hearts back to Him.
author: Elijah
text:
O Lord, God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel,
let it be known this day,
that you are God in Israel,
and that I am your servant,
and that I have done all these things at your word.

Answer me, O Lord, answer me,
that this people may know,
that you, O Lord, are God,
and that you have turned their hearts back.

title: Psalm 51 – Miserere (Have Mercy on Me, O God)
shortDescription: King David's heartfelt prayer of repentance from Psalm 51 – a plea for God's mercy and cleansing from sin, acknowledging his transgressions and seeking a renewed heart and spirit.
author: King David
text:
Have mercy on me, O God,
according to your steadfast love;

according to your abundant mercy
blot out my transgressions.

Wash me thoroughly from my iniquity,
and cleanse me from my sin!

For I know my transgressions,
and my sin is ever before me.

Against you, you only, have I sinned
and done what is evil in your sight,

so that you may be justified in your words
and blameless in your judgment.

Create in me a clean heart, O God,
and renew a right spirit within me.

Cast me not away from your presence,
and take not your Holy Spirit from me.

Restore to me the joy of your salvation,
and uphold me with a willing spirit.

title: Psalm 103 – Bless the Lord, O My Soul
shortDescription: A traditional Catholic devotion, the Angelus prayer commemorates the Annunciation and Incarnation of Christ through scriptural verses and repetitions of the Hail Mary, concluding with a petition for grace through His Passion and Resurrection.
author: King David
text:
The Angel of the Lord declared unto Mary,
And she conceived of the Holy Spirit.

Hail Mary, full of grace, the Lord is with thee;
blessed art thou amongst women,
and blessed is the fruit of thy womb, Jesus.
Holy Mary, Mother of God,
pray for us sinners,
now and at the hour of our death.
Amen.

Behold the handmaid of the Lord,
Be it done unto me according to thy word.

Hail Mary, full of grace, the Lord is with thee;
blessed art thou amongst women,
and blessed is the fruit of thy womb, Jesus.
Holy Mary, Mother of God,
pray for us sinners,
now and at the hour of our death.
Amen.

And the Word was made Flesh,
And dwelt among us.

Hail Mary, full of grace, the Lord is with thee;
blessed art thou amongst women,
and blessed is the fruit of thy womb, Jesus.
Holy Mary, Mother of God,
pray for us sinners,
now and at the hour of our death.
Amen.

Pray for us, O holy Mother of God,
That we may be made worthy of the promises of Christ.

Let us pray:
Pour forth, we beseech Thee, O Lord, Thy grace into our hearts;
that we, to whom the incarnation of Christ, Thy Son,
was made known by the message of an angel,
may by His Passion and Cross
be brought to the glory of His Resurrection.
Through the same Christ our Lord.

Amen.

title: Psalm 150 – Praise the Lord
shortDescription: King David's Psalm 150 – a vibrant call for all creation to praise God with every instrument and every breath, celebrating His mighty deeds and excellent greatness.
author: King David

title: Psalm 143 – A Prayer in Distress
shortDescription: King David's prayer from the Psalms – a heartfelt plea for God's mercy and faithfulness, expressing a deep spiritual thirst and a desire for divine guidance and steadfast love.
author: King David
text:
Hear my prayer, O Lord;
give ear to my pleas for mercy!
In your faithfulness answer me,
in your righteousness!

My soul thirsts for you like a parched land.

Let me hear in the morning of your steadfast love,
for in you I trust.

Make me know the way I should go,
for to you I lift up my soul.

title: Psalm 130 – De Profundis (Out of the Depths)
shortDescription: King David's prayer from the depths of despair – a fervent plea for God's mercy and a declaration of patient hope in His word.
author: King David
text:
Out of the depths I cry to you, O Lord!

O Lord, hear my voice!

Let your ears be attentive to the voice of my pleas for mercy!

I wait for the Lord, my soul waits,
and in his word I hope;

my soul waits for the Lord
more than watchmen for the morning.

title: Kaddish (Jewish Prayer of Sanctification)
shortDescription: The Kaddish, a central prayer in Jewish tradition – a profound declaration of God's magnified and sanctified Name, expressing a fervent hope for the swift establishment of His kingdom and eternal blessing.
author: Jewish Tradition
text:
Magnified and sanctified be His great Name
in the world which He has created according to His will.

May He establish His kingdom in your lifetime and in your days,
and within the life of the entire House of Israel, speedily and soon;
and say, Amen.

May His great Name be blessed forever and to all eternity.

Blessed and praised, glorified and exalted, extolled and honoured, adored and lauded be the Name of the Holy Blessed One,
beyond all the blessings and hymns, praises and consolations that are ever spoken in the world;
and say, Amen.

title: Cranmer's Collect for Holy Communion
shortDescription: Thomas Cranmer's Collect for Purity from the 1549 Book of Common Prayer – a plea to the all-knowing God for the Holy Spirit to cleanse hearts, enabling perfect love and worthy magnification of God's holy Name.
author: Thomas Cranmer
text:
Almighty God, unto whom all hearts are open,
all desires known, and from whom no secrets are hid;

Cleanse the thoughts of our hearts
by the inspiration of thy Holy Spirit,
that we may perfectly love thee,
and worthily magnify thy holy Name;
through Christ our Lord.

Amen.

title: Calvin's Morning Prayer
shortDescription: John Calvin's morning prayer from 1542 – a thanksgiving for a peaceful night and a petition to dedicate all daily thoughts, words, and deeds to God's glory and the edification of neighbors.
author: John Calvin
text:
My God, Father and Saviour,
since thou hast been pleased to give me grace
to pass this night in quiet,
and hast preserved me from all evil and danger,
let it please thee also to grant
that throughout this day thy divine majesty
may be glorified by me;

that I may employ all my thoughts, words, and deeds
to thy service and the edification of my neighbours;
so that all I do may tend to the honour of thy name.

title: Thomas More – Prayer for Enemies
shortDescription: Thomas More's prayer from 1535 – a profound plea for the grace to love enemies, pray for persecutors, and overcome bitterness with good, mirroring Christ's example of forgiveness even for those who crucified him.
author: Thomas More
text:
Lord, give me the grace to love my enemies
and to pray for those who persecute me.

Take from me all bitterness and unkindness.

Help me to overcome evil with good,
and to do good unto those who hate me.

Grant me to follow the example of our Saviour,
who prayed for those who crucified him:
Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do.

title: Thomas More – Prayer for Patience in Suffering (Tower of London)
shortDescription: Thomas More's prayer from 1534–1535 – a profound request for a longing for God for His own sake, seeking virtues like humility and charity, and the grace to contemplate Christ's passion while detaching from worldly concerns.
author: Thomas More

title: Prayer of Abandonment (Charles de Foucauld)
shortDescription: Charles de Foucauld's prayer of abandonment – a complete surrender of self into God's hands, accepting all with boundless confidence and profound filial love, desiring only for God's will to be done.
author: Charles de Foucauld
text:
Father,
I abandon myself into your hands;
do with me what you will.

Whatever you may do, I thank you;
I am ready for all,
I accept all.

Let only your will be done in me,
and in all your creatures.

I wish no more than this, O Lord.

Into your hands I commend my spirit;
I offer it to you with all the love of my heart,
for I love you, Lord,
and so need to give myself,
to surrender myself into your hands without reserve,
and with boundless confidence,
for you are my Father.

title: Puritan Prayer – The Precious Blood (Valley of Vision)
shortDescription: A Puritan prayer from the 17th–18th century, expressing deep repentance and despair over sin, but finding complete hope and reliance in the atoning blood and perfect righteousness of Jesus Christ. It emphasizes justification by Christ's merit alone.
author: Puritan Tradition
text:
O Lord,
my sins are many and great,
but the blood of Jesus Christ cleanses from all sin.

When I consider the burden of my guilt,
I despair of standing before you.

But when I look to your Son,
to his perfect life and atoning death,
to his resurrection and intercession,
I take heart.

His blood is more powerful than my sin.
His righteousness covers my shame.

I plead nothing of my own before your throne,
but cast myself wholly on his merit and his mercy.

title: Puritan Prayer – The Best Robe (Valley of Vision)
shortDescription: A prayer from the Puritan tradition, echoing the parable of the Prodigal Son – the speaker returns from a life of sin, burdened by guilt, yet is met with God's immediate, loving forgiveness and full restoration.
author: Puritan Tradition
text:
O God,
I return again from the far country,
laden with guilt and shame.

I have wasted your inheritance,
yet you see me while I am yet far off,
and run to meet me,
and wrap around me the best robe.

You put a ring on my hand
and shoes on my feet,
and my heart is shamed and glad at once.

I was lost and am found;
I was dead and am alive.

title: Prayer from the Book of Common Prayer – O Merciful Father
shortDescription: An Anglican prayer from the 1662 tradition – a compassionate plea to God for a suffering servant, asking for mercy, comfort, healing, and strength to live faithfully despite affliction.
author: Anglican Tradition
text:
O merciful Father,
who hast taught us in thy holy word
that thou dost not willingly afflict or grieve
the children of men:

Look with pity upon the sorrows of thy servant
for whom our prayers are offered.
Look upon him with the eyes of thy mercy;
comfort him with a sense of thy goodness;
preserve him from the temptations of the enemy;
and give him patience under his affliction.

Restore him, we pray,
to health of body and mind,
and grant him to live the rest of his life
in thy fear and to thy glory.

title: Orthodox Prayer for the Sick and Dying
shortDescription: An Orthodox prayer from the tradition – a plea to God, the healer of all sickness, to visit and restore a sick servant in both soul and body, that they may glorify His holy Name with a grateful heart.
author: Orthodox Tradition
text:
O Lord our God,
who healest every sickness and every weakness,
who hast power over life and death:
visit, we pray thee, in thy goodness
this thy servant who lies sick.

Stretch out thy mighty hand,
full of healing and relief,
and heal him.

Grant him healing of soul and body;
and make him worthy to glorify thy holy Name
with a grateful heart,
through Christ our Lord.

Amen.

title: Stay Close to Me, Lord
shortDescription: A heartfelt personal prayer for divine companionship and guidance, seeking the courage to live authentically and to manifest God's light and love, inspired by the example of another.
text:
Wherever you lead me today,
stay close to me, Lord.

Give me the courage to speak honestly
and to show what is true within me.

And when my words are not enough,
let your light be visible in my eyes
and your love felt in my heart.

For that is how I first recognized that spark myself —
the one that warms my soul today:
in the life of someone who was like me.

Stay close to me, Lord,
wherever you lead me today.

Amen.

title: An Evening Prayer of Trust
shortDescription: A contemporary evening prayer reflecting on the day, offering gratitude for blessings and entrusting unresolved matters to God. It seeks peace for the night and renewed light for the morning.
text:
Lord, the day is behind me.

What was loud may now grow quiet.

What remains unresolved, I place in your hands.

I thank you for everything that carried me today:

for the people I encountered,

for small signs of kindness,

for strength at the right moment,

for stillness where I did not expect it.

I bring you the heavy things too.

Not everything do I understand,

not everything can I resolve.

But I am allowed to let go.

Stay with me through this night.

Watch over my heart, over my thoughts,

and over all those entrusted to me.

Let me sleep in your peace

and wake tomorrow in your light.

Amen.

title: A Quiet Evening Prayer
shortDescription: A personal evening prayer of gratitude for the day's blessings and connections, acknowledging faults and surrendering burdens. It seeks God's peace and assurance of being held through the night.
text:
Good God, before I close my eyes,
I come to you once more.

Not with many words,
but with a quiet thank you.

Thank you for this day.
For what went well.
For what moved me.
For people who were close to me —
even from a distance.
Thank you for bread, for shelter, for breath,
for the small things that are easily overlooked.

What I did wrong today,
I lay before you.
What weighs on me,
I give back to you.
What I do not need to carry,
I let go.

Be my peace in this night.
Lay your stillness over my heart
and remind me that I am held —
even while I sleep.

Amen.

title: A Prayer of Creation's Wonder
shortDescription: A personal prayer expressing gratitude for the beauty and quiet wonders of God's creation – from light and wind to soil and life. It seeks open eyes to perceive the holy in the simple and God's presence in all things, and asks for grace to love and care for the earth with reverence.
text:
Lord, I stand still before the beauty of your creation.

Before the light that falls through the trees,
before the wind that moves across the earth,
before the sky that stretches wider than my thinking.

I thank you for the quiet wonders that surround me:
for soil and water,
for animals and plants,
for color, for breath, for life.

Do not let me pass through your world without noticing.
Open my eyes to the holy in the simple,
to your presence in everything that grows,
sounds, shines, and lives.
And teach me to love this earth with reverence
and to care for it with devotion.

Amen.

title: Courage for the Next Step
shortDescription: A personal prayer seeking God's courage and strength to navigate daily challenges – asking for trust, light, and renewed vigor, and emphasizing reliance on divine presence for each step forward in peace.
text:
Lord,
give me courage
for what lies before me today.

Not the courage to control everything,
but the courage to take the next step.

Strengthen my heart
when doubt grows loud.
Stay with me
when I am uncertain.
Remind me
that I do not walk alone.

Let me be honest in what I say,
clear in what I do,
and open to what you want to show me.

When fear holds me back,
give me trust.
When the path is unclear,
give me light.
When I grow weary,
give me new strength.

Lead me forward,
step by step,
in your peace.

Amen.

title: Walking with God Through Fear
shortDescription: A personal prayer from an individual facing fear, acknowledging life's difficulties while maintaining faith. It asks for God's steadfast presence and companionship through hesitant steps, expressing quiet hope for divine support.
text:
God, I am frightened right now.

Not because I have lost my faith —
but because life is sometimes harder
than I had imagined.

I am not asking you to simply take the fear away.
I am asking you to stand beside me
while I walk anyway.

One step. Then another.
Help me not only to know this today,
but to feel it.

I am setting out —
with a heart that hesitates,
and the quiet hope
that you are there.

Amen.

title: Suscipe (Take, Lord, Receive All My Liberty)
shortDescription: Ignatius of Loyola's prayer of total self-offering – surrendering all personal faculties and possessions back to God, asking only for His love and grace as sufficient.
author: Ignatius of Loyola
text:
Take, Lord, and receive all my liberty,
my memory, my understanding,
and my entire will,
all I have and call my own.

You have given all to me.
To you, Lord, I return it.

Everything is yours; do with it what you will.
Give me only your love and your grace,
that is enough for me.

title: Teach Us to Pray (Ignatius of Loyola)
shortDescription: Ignatius of Loyola's prayer for selfless service – a plea to be taught generosity, to serve God without counting the cost or seeking reward, but only to do His will.
author: Ignatius of Loyola
text:
Dearest Lord, teach me to be generous;

teach me to serve you as you deserve;

to give and not to count the cost,

to fight and not to heed the wounds,

to toil and not to seek for rest,

to labor and not to ask for reward,

save that of knowing that I do your will.

title: Prayer of Saint Richard of Chichester
shortDescription: Richard of Chichester's prayer of profound gratitude to Jesus for his sacrifice, expressing a deep desire to know, love, and follow Christ more closely each day.
author: Richard of Chichester
text:
Thanks be to thee, my Lord Jesus Christ,
for all the benefits thou hast given me,
for all the pains and insults thou hast borne for me.

O most merciful redeemer, friend and brother,
may I know thee more clearly,
love thee more dearly,
and follow thee more nearly,
day by day.

title: Lorica of Saint Patrick (Breastplate Prayer)
shortDescription: Saint Patrick's powerful invocation for divine protection and guidance, drawing strength from both creation and the omnipresent Christ in all aspects of life.
author: Saint Patrick
text:
I arise today
through the strength of heaven,
the light of the sun,
the radiance of the moon,
the splendor of fire,
the speed of lightning,
the swiftness of wind,
the depth of the sea,
the stability of the earth,
the firmness of rock.

I arise today
through God's strength to pilot me,
God's might to uphold me,
God's wisdom to guide me,
God's eye to look before me,
God's ear to hear me,
God's word to speak for me,
God's hand to guard me,
God's shield to protect me,
God's host to save me.

Christ with me,
Christ before me,
Christ behind me,
Christ in me,
Christ beneath me,
Christ above me,
Christ on my right,
Christ on my left,
Christ when I lie down,
Christ when I sit down,
Christ when I arise,
Christ in the heart of every man who thinks of me,
Christ in the mouth of everyone who speaks of me,
Christ in every eye that sees me,
Christ in every ear that hears me.

title: The Examen of Conscience (Ignatius of Loyola)
shortDescription: Ignatius of Loyola's daily examen – a prayer of reflection at the close of day, offering thanks for gifts, seeking forgiveness for failures, and surrendering the past while asking for renewed trust and a spirit of love for the coming day.
author: Ignatius of Loyola
text:
Lord, I turn to you at the close of this day
and ask for the grace to see your hand at work in all that has happened.

I give you thanks for the moments of beauty, love, and joy I received —
I did not earn them; they were gifts.

I ask forgiveness for the moments I failed you and others:
for the unkind word, the missed opportunity, the careless act,
the pride I would not lay down.

Help me to see where you were calling me and where I looked away.
Renew in me a spirit of trust,
that tomorrow I may walk more faithfully in your light.

I place this day in your hands
and surrender what I cannot undo.
Grant me rest,
and let me wake tomorrow ready to love as you love.

Amen.

title: O Gracious Light (Phos Hilaron)
shortDescription: An early Christian evening hymn from ancient tradition, this prayer praises Jesus Christ as the gracious Light and pure brightness of God, acknowledging the Holy Trinity as worthy of perpetual glorification at the close of day.
author: Early Christian Tradition
text:
O gracious Light,
pure brightness of the everliving Father in heaven,
O Jesus Christ, holy and blessed!

Now as we come to the setting of the sun,
and our eyes behold the vesper light,
we sing your praises, O God: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

You are worthy at all times to be praised by happy voices,
O Son of God, O Giver of life,
and to be glorified through all the worlds.

title: A Morning Resolve (John Wesley)
shortDescription: John Wesley's prayer of consecration – a complete surrender of the self to God's will, embracing all circumstances as part of a divine covenant.
author: John Wesley
text:
I am no longer my own, but thine.

Put me to what thou wilt,
rank me with whom thou wilt.

Put me to doing,
put me to suffering.

Let me be employed by thee or laid aside for thee,
exalted for thee or brought low by thee.

Let me be full,
let me be empty.

Let me have all things,
let me have nothing.

I freely and heartily yield all things
to thy pleasure and disposal.

And now, O glorious and blessed God,
Father, Son, and Holy Spirit,
thou art mine, and I am thine.

So be it.

And the covenant which I have made on earth,
let it be ratified in heaven.

Amen.

title: Prayer of Saint Bonaventure
shortDescription: Saint Bonaventure's fervent prayer for an all-consuming love for Jesus, expressing a deep longing to be pierced by divine love. It is a plea to hunger and thirst for Christ as the ultimate source of spiritual nourishment, wisdom, and eternal life.
author: Saint Bonaventure
text:
Pierce, O most sweet Lord Jesus,
my inmost soul with the most joyous and healthful wound of thy love,
with true, serene, and most holy apostolic charity,
that my soul may ever languish and melt with love and longing for thee,
that it may yearn for thee and faint for thy courts,
and long to be dissolved and to be with thee.

Grant that my soul may hunger after thee,
the bread of angels, the refreshment of holy souls,
our daily and supersubstantial bread,
having all sweetness and savor and every delightful taste.

Let my heart ever hunger after and feed upon thee,
upon whom the angels desire to look,
and may my inmost soul be filled with the sweetness of thy savor;
let it ever thirst for thee, the fountain of life,
the fountain of wisdom and knowledge, the fountain of eternal light,
the torrent of pleasure, the richness of the house of God.

title: Sweet Jesus, Body and Blood Most Holy (Bernard of Clairvaux)
shortDescription: Bernard of Clairvaux's hymn expresses profound personal devotion to Jesus, celebrating the ineffable sweetness of His name and presence. It is a heartfelt longing for Christ to be our ultimate hope, joy, and eternal glory.
author: Bernard of Clairvaux
text:
Jesus, the very thought of thee
with sweetness fills my breast;
but sweeter far thy face to see,
and in thy presence rest.

Nor voice can sing, nor heart can frame,
nor can the memory find
a sweeter sound than thy blest name,
O Saviour of mankind!

O hope of every contrite heart,
O joy of all the meek,
to those who fall, how kind thou art!
how good to those who seek!

But what to those who find? Ah, this
nor tongue nor pen can show;
the love of Jesus, what it is,
none but his loved ones know.

Jesus, our only joy be thou,
as thou our prize wilt be;
Jesus, be thou our glory now,
and through eternity.

title: All Shall Be Well (Julian of Norwich)
shortDescription: Julian of Norwich's famous words of comfort – an assurance that despite trials and afflictions, all will ultimately be well, rooted in God's enduring love and our steadfast trust in Him.
author: Julian of Norwich
text:
All shall be well,
and all shall be well,
and all manner of thing shall be well.

He said not, Thou shalt not be tempested,
thou shalt not be travailed,
thou shalt not be afflicted;
but He said: Thou shalt not be overcome.

God willeth that we take heed of these words,
and that we be ever mighty in sure trust, in weal and woe.
For He loveth and enjoyeth us,
and so willeth He that we love and enjoy Him
and mightily trust in Him;
and all shall be well.

title: Evening Prayer (Lancelot Andrewes)
shortDescription: Lancelot Andrewes' evening prayer – a blessing to God as the Creator of heaven and earth, offering evening praise and seeking divine guidance through the night for peaceful rest and rising to His glory.
author: Lancelot Andrewes
text:
Blessed art thou, O Lord our God,
the God of our fathers,
who hast made the heavens and the earth,
with all their array;
who hast made the day for the works of light,
and the night for the refreshment of our infirmity.

O loving Lord and Lover of mankind,
mercifully accept now the evening sacrifice of our praise.

As thou hast conducted us through the day
and brought us to the night's rest,
so lead us through the night to the calm of morning;
and grant us at the last to lie down in peace
and rise again, for the glory of thy name.

For thine is the greatness and the power,
the glory, the victory and the majesty:
all that is in heaven and in earth is thine.

Amen.

title: Morning Prayer (Lancelot Andrewes)
shortDescription: Lancelot Andrewes' morning prayer – a request for God's protection from sin and danger throughout the day, seeking divine guidance for righteous living.
author: Lancelot Andrewes

title: Lord, I Come Before Thee (George Herbert)
shortDescription: George Herbert's poem 'Love (III)' presents a dialogue between a soul burdened by sin and divine Love, who gently but firmly overcomes the soul's feelings of unworthiness to offer welcome and communion – a profound testament to grace and unconditional acceptance.
author: George Herbert
text:
Love bade me welcome; yet my soul drew back,

guilty of dust and sin.

But quick-eyed Love, observing me grow slack

from my first entrance in,

drew nearer to me, sweetly questioning

if I lacked anything.

'A guest,' I answered, 'worthy to be here.'

Love said, 'You shall be he.'

'I, the unkind, ungrateful? Ah, my dear,

I cannot look on thee.'

Love took my hand, and smiling did reply,

'Who made the eyes but I?'

'Truth, Lord, but I have marred them;

let my shame go where it doth deserve.'

'And know you not,' says Love, 'who bore the blame?'

'My dear, then I will serve.'

'You must sit down,' says Love, 'and taste my meat.'

So I did sit and eat.

title: A Hymn to God the Father (John Donne)
shortDescription: John Donne's deeply personal prayer is a profound confession of persistent sinfulness, seeking forgiveness for original sin, ongoing transgressions, and sins that led others astray. It culminates in a fervent plea for divine assurance of salvation and grace at the moment of death.
author: John Donne
text:
Wilt thou forgive that sin where I begun,
which was my sin, though it were done before?
Wilt thou forgive that sin through which I run,
and do run still, though still I do deplore?
When thou hast done, thou hast not done,
for I have more.

Wilt thou forgive that sin which I have won
others to sin, and made my sin their door?
Wilt thou forgive that sin which I did shun
a year or two, but wallowed in a score?
When thou hast done, thou hast not done,
for I have more.

I have a sin of fear, that when I have spun
my last thread, I shall perish on the shore;
swear by thyself that at my death thy Son
shall shine as he shines now, and heretofore;
and having done that, thou hast done,
I fear no more.

title: Prayer of William Penn
shortDescription: William Penn's prayer for humility and acceptance of God's will, especially concerning the return of loved ones to God. It is a profound expression of hope in eternal love and Christ's victory over death, enabling believers to face loss with quiet confidence.
author: William Penn
text:
O God, help us not to despise or oppose what we do not understand.

We give back to you, O God,
those whom you gave to us.

You did not lose them when you gave them to us,
and we do not lose them by their return to you.

Your dear Son taught us by his words and life
that love is eternal.

We pray that we may know this with our minds
and believe it in our hearts,

that even at the open grave we may not grieve
as those who have no hope,

but in quietness and confidence may still say,
'Thanks be to God,
who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.'

title: Prayer for Those in Need (William Temple)
shortDescription: William Temple's compassionate intercession for those experiencing deep suffering – loneliness, anxiety, grief, and lost hope or love. It asks for God's all-sufficient love and peace, through the Holy Spirit, to comfort them where human help is absent.
author: William Temple

title: O Lord, Support Us (John Henry Newman)
shortDescription: John Henry Newman's poignant prayer for God's sustaining support through life's difficulties — a plea for mercy to grant safe lodging, holy rest, and ultimate peace at the end of one's earthly journey.
author: John Henry Newman
text:
O Lord, support us all the day long of this troublous life,
until the shadows lengthen and the evening comes,
and the busy world is hushed,
and the fever of life is over,
and our work is done.

Then, Lord, in thy mercy,
grant us safe lodging,
a holy rest,
and peace at the last.

Through Jesus Christ our Lord.

Amen.

title: Lead, Kindly Light (John Henry Newman)
shortDescription: John Henry Newman's hymn is a humble plea for divine guidance through life's darkness, acknowledging past pride and seeking just enough light for the next step – a testament to trust in God's power until reaching eternal peace.
author: John Henry Newman
text:
Lead, Kindly Light, amid the encircling gloom,
lead thou me on!

The night is dark, and I am far from home;
lead thou me on!

Keep thou my feet;
I do not ask to see the distant scene —
one step enough for me.

I was not ever thus,
nor prayed that thou shouldst lead me on;

I loved to choose and see my path;
but now lead thou me on!

I loved the garish day, and, spite of fears,
pride ruled my will.
Remember not past years!

So long thy power hath blessed me,
sure it still will lead me on

o'er moor and fen,
o'er crag and torrent, till the night is gone,

and with the morn those angel faces smile,
which I have loved long since, and lost awhile.

title: Prayer Before a Crucifix (attr. Thomas Aquinas)
shortDescription: A prayer by Thomas Aquinas, where the supplicant kneels before Jesus, fervently asking for the virtues of faith, hope, and charity, along with true repentance and a desire for amendment. It centers on a deep contemplation of Christ's five wounds, recalling the prophetic words of David.
author: Thomas Aquinas
text:
Behold, O kind and most sweet Jesus,
I cast myself upon my knees in thy sight,
and with the most fervent desire of my soul,
I pray and beseech thee that thou wouldst impress
upon my heart lively sentiments of faith, hope, and charity,
with a true repentance for my sins,
and a firm desire of amendment,
while with deep affection and grief of soul
I ponder within myself and mentally contemplate
thy five most precious wounds;

having before my eyes that which David spoke in prophecy of thee,
O good Jesus:
They have pierced my hands and my feet;
they have numbered all my bones.

title: Nunc Dimittis (Canticle of Simeon)
shortDescription: Simeon's prayer from the Gospel of Luke, known as the Nunc Dimittis – a peaceful declaration upon seeing the infant Jesus, recognizing him as the salvation prepared for all nations and the glory of Israel.
author: Simeon
text:
Lord, now let your servant go in peace;

your word has been fulfilled.

My own eyes have seen the salvation

which you have prepared in the sight of every people:

a light to reveal you to the nations

and the glory of your people Israel.

title: Psalm 23 – The Lord Is My Shepherd
shortDescription: King David's Psalm 23 – a profound declaration of trust in God as a divine shepherd who provides, protects, and comforts the believer through all of life's challenges and into eternal dwelling.
author: King David
text:
The Lord is my shepherd;
I shall not want.

He makes me lie down in green pastures.
He leads me beside still waters.
He restores my soul.
He leads me in paths of righteousness
for his name's sake.

Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death,
I will fear no evil,
for you are with me;
your rod and your staff,
they comfort me.

You prepare a table before me
in the presence of my enemies;
you anoint my head with oil;
my cup overflows.
Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me
all the days of my life,
and I shall dwell in the house of the Lord
forever.

title: Psalm 121 – I Lift Up My Eyes to the Hills
shortDescription: King David's prayer from Psalm 121 – a declaration of trust in the Lord, the creator of heaven and earth, who is an ever-vigilant keeper and protector from all harm, both day and night, now and forevermore.
author: King David
text:
I lift up my eyes to the hills.

From where does my help come?

My help comes from the Lord,

who made heaven and earth.

He will not let your foot be moved;

he who keeps you will not slumber.

Behold, he who keeps Israel

will neither slumber nor sleep.

The Lord is your keeper;

the Lord is your shade on your right hand.

The sun shall not strike you by day,

nor the moon by night.

The Lord will keep you from all evil;

he will keep your life.

The Lord will keep

your going out and your coming in

from this time forth and forevermore.

title: Psalm 46 – God Is Our Refuge
shortDescription: A psalm by the Sons of Korah, this prayer declares God as an ever-present refuge and strength, urging trust in His power to bring peace and stillness amidst global turmoil and the earth's upheaval.
author: Sons of Korah
text:
God is our refuge and strength,
a very present help in trouble.

Therefore we will not fear though the earth gives way,
though the mountains be moved into the heart of the sea,
though its waters roar and foam,
though the mountains tremble at its swelling.

There is a river whose streams make glad the city of God,
the holy habitation of the Most High.
God is in the midst of her; she shall not be moved;
God will help her when morning dawns.

The Lord of hosts is with us;
the God of Jacob is our fortress.

Come, behold the works of the Lord,
how he has brought desolations on the earth.
He makes wars cease to the end of the earth;
he breaks the bow and shatters the spear;
he burns the chariots with fire.
'Be still, and know that I am God.
I will be exalted among the nations,
I will be exalted in the earth!'

The Lord of hosts is with us;
the God of Jacob is our fortress.

title: Psalm 91 – He Who Dwells in the Shelter of the Most High
shortDescription: An anonymous prayer from Psalm 91 – a declaration of trust in God as a refuge and fortress, promising divine protection from all dangers and the presence of angels for those who dwell in Him.
author: Anonymous
text:
He who dwells in the shelter of the Most High
will abide in the shadow of the Almighty.

I will say to the Lord, 'My refuge and my fortress,
my God, in whom I trust.'

For he will deliver you from the snare of the fowler
and from the deadly pestilence.

He will cover you with his pinions,
and under his wings you will find refuge;
his faithfulness is a shield and buckler.

You will not fear the terror of the night,
nor the arrow that flies by day,
nor the pestilence that stalks in darkness,
nor the destruction that wastes at noonday.

Because you have made the Lord your dwelling place —
the Most High, who is my refuge —
no evil shall be allowed to befall you,
no plague come near your tent.

For he will command his angels concerning you
to guard you in all your ways.

On their hands they will bear you up,
lest you strike your foot against a stone.

Because he holds fast to me in love, I will deliver him;
I will protect him, because he knows my name.

When he calls to me, I will answer him;
I will be with him in trouble;
I will rescue him and honor him.

With long life I will satisfy him
and show him my salvation.

title: A General Thanksgiving (Book of Common Prayer, 1662)
shortDescription: A general thanksgiving from the Book of Common Prayer, offering humble thanks for creation, preservation, and above all, for God's redemptive love through Jesus Christ. It prays for genuine gratitude that manifests not only in words but in a life of devoted service, holiness, and righteousness.
author: Book of Common Prayer
text:
Almighty God, Father of all mercies,
we thine unworthy servants
do give thee most humble and hearty thanks
for all thy goodness and loving-kindness
to us and to all men.

We bless thee for our creation, preservation,
and all the blessings of this life;
but above all for thine inestimable love
in the redemption of the world by our Lord Jesus Christ,
for the means of grace, and for the hope of glory.

And we beseech thee, give us that due sense
of all thy mercies,
that our hearts may be unfeignedly thankful,
and that we shew forth thy praise,
not only with our lips,
but in our lives;
by giving up ourselves to thy service,
and by walking before thee
in holiness and righteousness all our days;
through Jesus Christ our Lord,
to whom with thee and the Holy Ghost
be all honour and glory,
world without end.

Amen.

title: Evening Collect (Book of Common Prayer)
shortDescription: From the Book of Common Prayer, this prayer is a dual plea for divine protection and peace during the night – asking God to lighten darkness, defend from dangers, drive away evil, and for holy angels to dwell and preserve peace.
author: Book of Common Prayer

title: Prayer of Thomas à Kempis
shortDescription: Thomas à Kempis's prayer for divine wisdom and discernment – a plea to align one's knowledge, love, and judgment with God's will, and to humbly navigate human interactions.
author: Thomas à Kempis
text:
Grant me, O Lord, to know what I ought to know,
to love what I ought to love,
to praise what delights thee most,
to value what is precious in thy sight,
to hate what is offensive to thee.

Suffer me not to judge according to the sight of my eyes,
nor to pass sentence according to the hearing of the ears of ignorant men;
but to discern with true judgment between things visible and spiritual,
and above all things to inquire what is the good pleasure of thy will.

Grant me, too, wisely to avoid the man who flatters me,
and patiently to hear him who contradicts me;
for this is great wisdom, to let vain words pass by
and to attend humbly to an angry and impious censure.

Amen.

title: The Vesper Hymn (Te Lucis Ante Terminum)
shortDescription: From early Christian tradition, this prayer for the close of day asks God the Creator to be a guard and keeper through the night – defending from ill dreams, fears, and spiritual foes, and preserving purity. It concludes with a Trinitarian doxology.
author: Early Christian Tradition
text:
Before the ending of the day,
Creator of the world, we pray
that with thy wonted favor thou
wouldst be our guard and keeper now.

From all ill dreams defend our eyes,
from nightly fears and fantasies;
tread under foot our ghostly foe,
that no pollution we may know.

O Father, that we ask be done,
through Jesus Christ, thine only Son;
who, with the Holy Ghost and thee,
doth live and reign eternally.

Amen.

title: Prayer of Saint Chrysostom (Book of Common Prayer)
shortDescription: John Chrysostom's prayer for communal worship, acknowledging God's grace in gathering and his promise to answer collective supplications – it asks for desires to be fulfilled expediently, granting knowledge of truth in this world and everlasting life in the next.
author: John Chrysostom
text:
Almighty God,
who hast given us grace at this time with one accord
to make our common supplications unto thee;

and dost promise that when two or three
are gathered together in thy Name
thou wilt grant their requests:

Fulfil now, O Lord, the desires and petitions of thy servants,
as may be most expedient for them;

granting us in this world knowledge of thy truth,
and in the world to come life everlasting.

Amen.

title: Prayer Frame of Adoration, Confession, Thanksgiving and Supplication
shortDescription: A structured prayer framework with four elements: Adoration, Confession, Thanksgiving, and Supplication — a guide to complete and balanced prayer.
text:
Start by becoming still.
Take a moment to place yourself before God.

Silence.

God, I direct my attention to You.
You are holy, present, merciful, and faithful.
Before I ask for anything, I remember who You are.

Silence.

God, I come before You honestly.
I acknowledge where I have fallen short in thought, word, or deed.
I bring before You what I have avoided, hidden, or carried alone.

Silence.

God, I thank You for Your goodness.
For the life I have received,
for the people who carry me,
for the grace I so often overlook,
and for the quiet ways in which You are near.

Silence.

God, I bring my requests before You.
I ask for wisdom, strength, protection, and peace.
I pray for the people I love,
for those who suffer,
and for what I cannot solve on my own.

Silence.

Lord, receive this prayer.
Shape my heart,
guide my steps,
and help me to live in trust.

Amen.

title: Prayer Frame of Truth, Acceptance and Readiness
shortDescription: A three-step prayer framework: Tell God honestly what you are carrying; Accept his presence in it; and Receive his gift.
text:
God, here I am.
I don't need to have everything in order before I come to You.
I may say what is heavy,
what is on my mind,
what brings me joy,
what brings me shame,
and what I don't yet understand myself.

I speak before You
what needs space within me.

Silence.

God, come to meet me in what I need now.
Open my eyes where I am stuck.
Strengthen me where I am growing faint.
Touch what is wounded.
Bring order to what has become confused.
And give me the next step,
not the whole path at once.

I also ask You for the people
who are close to me today,
and for those I can only hold in silent thought.

Silence.

God, I lay before You
what I cannot hold on to.
I don't need to understand everything.
I don't need to solve everything right away.
I may wait, hope, and keep going.

Silence.

God, what I have said now lies before You.
What remains unspoken, You know.
Keep me in Your peace
and lead me through this day.

Amen.

title: Prayer Frame of Adoration, Listening, Thanksgiving, Asking and Resting
shortDescription: A five-step contemplative prayer framework as an inner altar: Adoration, Listening, Thankfulness, Ask, and Rest in God's presence.
text:
Start by becoming still.
Breathe quietly in and out.
Picture yourself stepping inwardly before God.

Not with performance.
Not with a mask.
But with an open heart.

Become aware:
You are now before God.

Silence.

God, You are greater than my thinking
and closer than my breath.
You are origin, light, and life.
All that is good finds its beginning in You.

I turn to You,
not first because of my questions,
but because You are God.

Silence.

Lord, I give You my attention.
I bring You not only words,
but my heart.

Make me still enough to perceive Your nearness.
Make me open enough to hear
what within me longs to grow quiet.

Awaken in me a love
that is more faithful than my mood,
deeper than my habit,
and quieter than my desire for control.

Draw me closer to You.

Silence.

God, I recognize
that my life consists not only of what is missing.

There is grace.
There is protection.
There are people, paths, moments,
and small signs of Your goodness.

Help me not to pass by Your gifts unnoticed.

Silence.

Lord, I open before You what needs help.

Come into my weakness.
Speak into my uncertainty.
Comfort what has grown weary.
Heal what has been wounded.

And bless the people I now entrust to You.

Silence.

God, I don't need to force anything right now.
I don't need to understand everything.
I don't need to know immediately how things will continue.

I may rest before You.
I may receive.
I may simply be, without needing to achieve anything.

Silence.

Lord, I do not leave You behind,
but continue with You.

Let this prayer keep resonating in my actions.
Guide my heart, my words, and my steps.

Amen.

title: Quiet Strength
shortDescription: A five-step prayer framework as a return to stillness: Others, Silence, Strength, Focus, and Thanks.
text:
God, before I bring anything of my own, I bring the people around me.
I hold up the ones who are struggling, the ones who are waiting, the ones I haven't thought of in a while.

Silence.

Now go quiet for a minute.
Don't ask for anything. Just listen, and notice whatever comes up.

Silence.

God, give me strength for what I'm carrying right now.
Point my body and my effort toward good, not toward harm.

Silence.

Bring to mind one thing you're especially grateful for today.
Sit with it, without rushing past it.

Silence.

Thank you for carrying me through this day.
Thank you for the people I hold, and for what I still don't understand.
Receive this prayer. Amen.
